relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: After many days of rest, Joshua's age comes to the front.
He gathers elders, heads, judges, and officers and names his own old age.
He asks them to remember what they have seen.
He speaks of remaining nations already assigned within tribal inheritance.
The future is still described through dispossession.
Their strength must become careful practice of Moses' Torah.
Covenant identity is guarded against adopting the worship of surrounding nations.
The people are told to cling to YHWH.
Joshua recalls victories over powerful nations.
One pursuing a thousand is explained through Divine battle help.
The deepest guard is love of YHWH with one's life.
Remaining nations and marriage ties are framed as covenant danger.
The warning turns neighbors into images of snare, trap, whip, and thorn.
Joshua prepares to go the way of all earth and testifies that the good word did not fall.
The speech insists that warning can arrive as surely as promise.
Serving other gods is pictured as covenant rupture and land loss.

contemplative_note: Joshua 23 is an old leader's warning speech. Its ethical reception must refuse ethnic-purity and religious-persecution uses while preserving the deeper question: what does a community cling to when the founder is leaving and the world around it remains mixed?
